seo.title: Competitive Programming Patterns in C++ - Two Pointers, Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Bit Manipulation | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master competitive programming patterns in C++: two pointers, sliding window, prefix sum, binary search on answer, bit manipulation, and greedy algorithms. All code is compilable. Includes 60+ coding and output-based questions.
seo.keywords: competitive programming c++, two pointers c++, sliding window c++, prefix sum c++, binary search on answer, bit manipulation c++, greedy algorithm c++, cp patterns, codeforces c++, leetcode c++

content.what:
What Are Competitive Programming Patterns?

Competitive programming patterns are reusable algorithmic templates that solve broad categories of problems efficiently. Instead of inventing a solution from scratch for every problem, experienced competitive programmers recognize the pattern and apply a known technique.

The most important patterns in competitive programming are:

  • Fast I/O -- critical for large inputs on online judges
  • Two Pointers -- scan from both ends or use two markers
  • Sliding Window -- maintain a window of elements that slides across the array
  • Prefix Sum -- precompute cumulative sums for O(1) range queries
  • Binary Search on Answer -- binary search the solution space, not the array
  • Bit Manipulation -- use bitwise operations for O(1) tricks
  • Greedy -- make the locally optimal choice at each step
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
using namespace std;

// Fast I/O setup (use in every CP solution)
void fastIO() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    cout.tie(nullptr);
}

int main() {
    fastIO();

    // Two pointers: find pair with given sum in sorted array
    vector<int> v = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11};
    int target = 12;
    int left = 0, right = v.size() - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        int sum = v[left] + v[right];
        if (sum == target) {
            cout << v[left] << " + " << v[right] << " = " << target << endl;
            break;
        } else if (sum < target) left++;
        else right--;
    }
    return 0;
}

When Siddharth says "this is a sliding window problem" or Ananya says "binary search on the answer," the pattern immediately narrows the solution approach and implementation time.
content.why:
Why Are Competitive Programming Patterns Important?

1. Speed in Contests

In a timed contest (Codeforces, CodeChef, ICPC), recognizing the pattern is 80% of solving the problem. If you see "find a subarray with maximum sum of length k," you immediately think sliding window. If you see "minimize the maximum value," you think binary search on answer. Pattern recognition saves critical minutes.

2. Reducing O(n^2) to O(n) or O(n log n)

Most competitive programming patterns exist to reduce brute-force time complexity. Two pointers reduces O(n^2) pair finding to O(n). Sliding window reduces O(n*k) subarray problems to O(n). Prefix sums reduce O(n) per range query to O(1). These optimizations are the difference between TLE and AC.

3. Structured Problem Solving

Patterns provide a mental framework: read the problem, identify constraints, match a pattern, implement the template. This structured approach works far better than ad-hoc coding, especially under pressure.

4. Interview Preparation

Top tech companies (Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart, Uber) heavily test these patterns. Two pointers, sliding window, prefix sums, and bit manipulation appear in the majority of coding interviews. Mastering these patterns directly translates to interview success.

5. Building Blocks for Advanced Algorithms

These basic patterns are building blocks for advanced techniques. Segment trees build on prefix sums. Convex hull trick builds on binary search. Network flow uses greedy and BFS. Mastering the basics makes advanced algorithms approachable.

codeExamples.title: Two Pointers: Container With Most Water
codeExamples.explanation: The two-pointer technique works on sorted arrays or problems where moving one pointer reduces the search space. Here, we start from both ends and always move the shorter side inward because keeping the shorter side cannot improve the area. This reduces O(n^2) brute force to O(n).

codeExamples.title: Sliding Window: Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K
codeExamples.explanation: The sliding window technique maintains a window of size k that slides one position at a time. Instead of recomputing the sum from scratch (O(k) per position), we add the new element and remove the old one (O(1) per position). Total complexity: O(n) instead of O(n*k).

codeExamples.title: Sliding Window: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
codeExamples.explanation: This is a variable-size sliding window. We expand the right end and shrink the left end when a duplicate is found within the current window. The lastSeen map tracks the most recent index of each character, allowing O(1) duplicate detection. Total: O(n) time, O(min(n, alphabet_size)) space.

codeExamples.title: Prefix Sum: Subarray Sum Equals K
codeExamples.explanation: This is the prefix sum + hash map pattern. If prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k, then the subarray from i+1 to j sums to k. We store prefix sum frequencies in a hash map. For each position, we check if (current_sum - k) exists as a previous prefix sum. This solves the problem in O(n) instead of O(n^2).

codeExamples.title: Binary Search on Answer: Minimum Maximum Pages Allocation
codeExamples.explanation: Binary search on the answer: instead of searching for an element, we binary search the solution space. Here the answer (maximum pages any student reads) lies between max(books) and sum(books). For each candidate answer, we check feasibility with a greedy allocation. Total: O(n log(sum)) time. This pattern appears in hundreds of competitive programming problems.

codeExamples.title: Bit Manipulation: Common Tricks
codeExamples.explanation: Bit manipulation provides O(1) operations for common tasks. Check bit: (n >> i) & 1. Set bit: n | (1 << i). Clear bit: n & ~(1 << i). Toggle bit: n ^ (1 << i). Power of 2: n & (n-1) == 0. XOR to find the unique element: pairs cancel out since a ^ a = 0. __builtin_popcount counts set bits in O(1).
codeExamples.output:
Bit 3 of 42: 1
After setting bit 0: 43
After clearing bit 1: 40
After toggling bit 3: 34
1 is power of 2
4 is power of 2
6 is not power of 2
8 is power of 2
16 is power of 2
15 is not power of 2
Set bits in 29: 4
Single number: 4

codeExamples.title: Greedy: Activity Selection Problem
codeExamples.explanation: The greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step. For activity selection, the greedy strategy is: sort by end time and always pick the next compatible activity (the one that starts after the current one ends). This provably gives the maximum number of non-overlapping activities. Greedy works when the problem has the greedy choice property and optimal substructure.

codeExamples.title: Greedy: Fractional Knapsack
codeExamples.explanation: Fractional knapsack is solvable by greedy: sort items by value-to-weight ratio and take as much of the highest-ratio item as possible. Unlike 0/1 knapsack (which requires dynamic programming), fractional knapsack allows taking fractions of items, making the greedy approach optimal.
codeExamples.output:
Take full item: value=60, weight=10
Take full item: value=100, weight=20
Take 66.6667% of item: value=120, weight=30
Total value: 240

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting Fast I/O (Causes TLE on Large Inputs)
commonMistakes.error: By default, cin/cout sync with C's scanf/printf, adding overhead. For inputs with 10^5+ elements, this can cause Time Limit Exceeded on online judges.
commonMistakes.explanation: ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false) disables synchronization between C and C++ I/O streams. cin.tie(nullptr) unties cin from cout so cout is not flushed before every cin. Add these two lines at the start of every competitive programming solution.

commonMistakes.title: Integer Overflow in Prefix Sum or Binary Search
commonMistakes.error: int stores up to ~2.1 billion. Prefix sums and binary search midpoints can exceed this, causing silent overflow and wrong answers.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use long long for prefix sums, cumulative products, and binary search bounds. Compute midpoint as lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2 to avoid overflow.

commonMistakes.title: Off-by-One in Sliding Window Size
commonMistakes.error: When sliding a window of size k, the element leaving the window is at index i-k, not i-k-1. Off-by-one errors are the most common bug in sliding window problems.
commonMistakes.explanation: When the window right end is at index i, the window contains elements from i-k+1 to i (inclusive). The element leaving is at index i-k. Always verify window boundaries with a small example.

commonMistakes.title: Binary Search on Answer with Wrong Bounds
commonMistakes.error: When lo = mid (not mid + 1), the search can get stuck in an infinite loop because lo never advances past mid when lo + 1 == hi.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use consistent binary search templates. The lo <= hi template with lo = mid + 1 and hi = mid - 1 is the safest and avoids infinite loops. Always move both pointers past mid.

commonMistakes.title: Using Bit Shift on Signed Negative Numbers
commonMistakes.error: Right-shifting a negative signed integer is implementation-defined in C++. The result depends on the compiler (arithmetic vs logical shift). This can cause wrong answers on different judges.
commonMistakes.explanation: For bit manipulation, prefer unsigned integers. Right-shifting unsigned values always fills with zeros (logical shift). If you must use signed integers, be aware that the behavior of right-shift on negatives varies by compiler.

summary: Fast I/O is mandatory in competitive programming. Add ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false) and cin.tie(nullptr) at the start of every solution. This alone can prevent TLE on problems with large inputs.

Two pointers technique uses two indices that move toward each other (or in the same direction) to solve problems in O(n). Classic applications: pair sum in sorted array, container with most water, removing duplicates, and merging sorted arrays.

Sliding window maintains a fixed or variable-size window that slides across the array. Fixed window: max sum subarray of size k. Variable window: longest substring without repeating characters. Both reduce O(n*k) or O(n^2) to O(n).
Prefix sum precomputes cumulative sums so that range sum queries are O(1): sum(l, r) = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. The prefix sum + hash map pattern solves subarray sum equals k in O(n).

Binary search on the answer binary searches the solution space instead of the array. The pattern is: define a range [lo, hi] for the answer, check feasibility with a greedy/simulation function, and narrow the range. Complexity: O(n log(answer_range)).

Bit manipulation provides O(1) operations: check bit ((n >> i) & 1), set bit (n | (1 << i)), clear bit (n & ~(1 << i)), toggle bit (n ^ (1 << i)), power of 2 check (n & (n-1) == 0), count set bits (__builtin_popcount).
XOR has three key properties: a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a, and XOR is commutative/associative. This allows finding the single non-duplicate element in O(n) time O(1) space by XORing all elements.

Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step. Activity selection: sort by end time, pick non-overlapping. Fractional knapsack: sort by value/weight ratio. Greedy works only when the problem has the greedy choice property.
Always use long long for prefix sums and binary search bounds to avoid integer overflow. Compute midpoint as lo + (hi - lo) / 2 instead of (lo + hi) / 2.

Time complexity analysis is essential: O(n) solutions handle n up to 10^7, O(n log n) handles up to 10^6, O(n^2) handles up to 10^4, and O(2^n) handles up to about 20. Always check constraints before choosing an approach.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

Competitive programming patterns are reusable algorithmic templates that solve broad categories of problems efficiently. Instead of inventing a solution from scratch for every problem, experienced competitive programmers recognize the pattern and apply a known technique.

The most important patterns in competitive programming are:

  • Fast I/O -- critical for large inputs on online judges
  • Two Pointers -- scan from both ends or use two markers
  • Sliding Window -- maintain a window of elements that slides across the array
  • Prefix Sum -- precompute cumulative sums for O(1) range queries
  • Binary Search on Answer -- binary search the solution space, not the array
  • Bit Manipulation -- use bitwise operations for O(1) tricks
  • Greedy -- make the locally optimal choice at each step
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
using namespace std;

// Fast I/O setup (use in every CP solution)
void fastIO() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    cout.tie(nullptr);
}

int main() {
    fastIO();

    // Two pointers: find pair with given sum in sorted array
    vector<int> v = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11};
    int target = 12;
    int left = 0, right = v.size() - 1;
    while (left < right) {
        int sum = v[left] + v[right];
        if (sum == target) {
            cout << v[left] << " + " << v[right] << " = " << target << endl;
            break;
        } else if (sum < target) left++;
        else right--;
    }
    return 0;
}

When Siddharth says "this is a sliding window problem" or Ananya says "binary search on the answer," the pattern immediately narrows the solution approach and implementation time.

1. Speed in Contests

In a timed contest (Codeforces, CodeChef, ICPC), recognizing the pattern is 80% of solving the problem. If you see "find a subarray with maximum sum of length k," you immediately think sliding window. If you see "minimize the maximum value," you think binary search on answer. Pattern recognition saves critical minutes.

2. Reducing O(n^2) to O(n) or O(n log n)

Most competitive programming patterns exist to reduce brute-force time complexity. Two pointers reduces O(n^2) pair finding to O(n). Sliding window reduces O(n*k) subarray problems to O(n). Prefix sums reduce O(n) per range query to O(1). These optimizations are the difference between TLE and AC.

3. Structured Problem Solving

Patterns provide a mental framework: read the problem, identify constraints, match a pattern, implement the template. This structured approach works far better than ad-hoc coding, especially under pressure.

4. Interview Preparation

Top tech companies (Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Flipkart, Uber) heavily test these patterns. Two pointers, sliding window, prefix sums, and bit manipulation appear in the majority of coding interviews. Mastering these patterns directly translates to interview success.

5. Building Blocks for Advanced Algorithms

These basic patterns are building blocks for advanced techniques. Segment trees build on prefix sums. Convex hull trick builds on binary search. Network flow uses greedy and BFS. Mastering the basics makes advanced algorithms approachable.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int n = 16;
cout << ((n & (n - 1)) == 0);
topicSpecific.hint: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. For powers of 2, this gives 0.
topicSpecific.explanation: 16 = 10000 in binary. n-1 = 15 = 01111. n & (n-1) = 0. Since the result is 0 and n > 0, it is a power of 2. Output: 1 (true).

topicSpecific.hint: XOR: a ^ a = 0, a ^ 0 = a. Pairs cancel out.
topicSpecific.explanation: 3 ^ 1 ^ 3 ^ 1 ^ 5 = (3^3) ^ (1^1) ^ 5 = 0 ^ 0 ^ 5 = 5. The XOR of pairs cancels out, leaving the unique element.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int n = 13;  // 1101 in binary
cout << __builtin_popcount(n);
topicSpecific.hint: __builtin_popcount counts the number of set bits (1s).
topicSpecific.explanation: 13 = 1101 in binary. There are three 1-bits. __builtin_popcount(13) = 3.

topicSpecific.hint: prefix[i] = sum of first i elements. Range sum [1,3] = prefix[4] - prefix[1].
topicSpecific.explanation: prefix = {0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 15}. prefix[4] - prefix[1] = 10 - 1 = 9. This is the sum of v[1]+v[2]+v[3] = 2+3+4 = 9.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int n = 10;  // 1010
int result = n | (1 << 0);  // Set bit 0
cout << result;
topicSpecific.hint: Setting bit 0 changes 1010 to 1011.
Two pointers on a sorted array. Start from both ends.
Sliding window of size 3. Track the maximum sum.
Extract each bit from MSB to LSB using right shift and AND with 1.
topicSpecific.explanation: 42 = 101010 in 6-bit binary. The loop extracts each bit from position 5 down to 0.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int n = 7;  // 0111
int cleared = n & ~(1 << 1);  // Clear bit 1
cout << cleared;

topicSpecific.hint: XOR swap trick: swaps two variables without a temporary.
topicSpecific.explanation: Step 1: a = 5^3 = 6. Step 2: b = 6^3 = 5. Step 3: a = 6^5 = 3. Values are swapped: a=3, b=5.

topicSpecific.hint: This generates all subsets using bitmask enumeration.
topicSpecific.explanation: Bitmask 000 to 111 generates all 2^3 = 8 subsets. Each bit position determines whether the corresponding element is included.

topicSpecific.hint: Kadane's algorithm finds the maximum subarray sum in O(n).
topicSpecific.explanation: Kadane's algorithm: at each position, either start a new subarray or extend the previous one. The maximum subarray is [4, -1, 2, 1] with sum 6.

topicSpecific.hint: Prefix sum + hash map to count subarrays with sum == k.
topicSpecific.explanation: Prefix sums: 0, 1, 2, 3. For sum=2: sum-k=0, prefixCount[0]=1 -> count=1. For sum=3: sum-k=1, prefixCount[1]=1 -> count=2. Two subarrays sum to 2: [1,1] (indices 0-1) and [1,1] (indices 1-2).

topicSpecific.hint: i & 1 checks if i is odd (bit 0 is set).
topicSpecific.explanation: i & 1 is 1 for odd numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9. That is 5 odd numbers in range [0, 9].

topicSpecific.hint: Sum of consecutive differences in an array.
topicSpecific.explanation: Differences: 10, 10, 10, 10. Sum = 40. This equals v[last] - v[first] = 50 - 10 = 40 (telescoping sum).

topicSpecific.hint: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. Count how many times until n becomes 0.
topicSpecific.explanation: 255 = 11111111 (8 set bits). Each iteration clears one bit. After 8 iterations, n = 0. This is Brian Kernighan's bit counting algorithm.

topicSpecific.hint: prefix[i] = sum of first i elements. Range sum v[1..3] = prefix[4] - prefix[1].
Binary search variant: find the last element <= target.
topicSpecific.explanation: After binary search: hi = 2 (index of 5, last element <= 6), lo = 3 (index of 7, first element > 6). This finds the upper bound of 6.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output?
int x = 5;  // 101
int y = 3;  // 011
cout << (x & y) << " " << (x | y);
topicSpecific.hint: AND keeps common bits. OR combines all bits.
Two pointers from both ends to check palindrome.
topicSpecific.explanation: "abcba" is a palindrome. Two pointers verify: a==a, b==b, c is the middle. isPalin remains true (1).

mixed.question:
What is the output?
int x = 6;  // 110
int y = 3;  // 011
cout << (x ^ y);
mixed.explanation: 110 XOR 011 = 101 = 5. XOR produces 1 in positions where the bits differ.

mixed.question:
What is the output?
int x = 12;
cout << (x >> 2);
mixed.hint: Right shift by 2 divides by 4 (for non-negative integers).
mixed.explanation: 12 = 1100. Right shift by 2: 0011 = 3. This is equivalent to integer division by 4.

mixed.question:
What is the output?
cout << (1 << 10);
mixed.hint: Left shift by k multiplies by 2^k.
mixed.explanation: 1 << 10 = 2^10 = 1024. Left shifting 1 by n positions gives 2^n.

mixed.hint: Two pointers counting all pairs that sum to target.
mixed.explanation: Pairs: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4) all sum to 7. The two-pointer approach finds all 3 pairs.

mixed.hint: prefix[i] stores sum of first i elements. Range sum = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l].

mixed.question:
What is the output?
int n = 20;  // 10100
int toggled = n ^ (1 << 2);  // Toggle bit 2
cout << toggled;
mixed.hint: XOR with 1 toggles a bit. 10100 XOR 00100 = ?
Find the minimum of maximum values across all windows of size k.
mixed.explanation: Windows of size 2: [4,2]->max=4, [2,1]->max=2, [1,5]->max=5, [5,3]->max=5. Minimum of these maxima = 2.

mixed.hint: n & (-n) isolates the lowest set bit.
mixed.explanation: 15 = 1111. -15 in two's complement = ...10001. 1111 & 10001 = 00001 = 1. The lowest set bit of 15 is bit 0, which has value 1.

mixed.hint: n & (-n) isolates the lowest set bit.
mixed.explanation: 12 = 1100. -12 in two's complement has the pattern such that 1100 & (-1100) = 0100 = 4. The lowest set bit is bit 2, which has value 4.

mixed.hint: Greedy coin change: use the largest coin as many times as possible.

mixed.question: When does the greedy approach work, and when does it fail?
mixed.hint: Think about the greedy choice property.
mixed.answer: Greedy works when the problem has the greedy choice property (a locally optimal choice leads to a globally optimal solution) and optimal substructure (optimal solution to the problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems). It works for activity selection, fractional knapsack, Huffman coding, and minimum spanning trees. It fails for 0/1 knapsack, shortest path with negative edges, and the general coin change problem (where coin denominations are not canonical).
mixed.explanation: For example, greedy coin change fails with coins {1, 3, 4} and amount 6. Greedy picks 4+1+1 = 3 coins, but optimal is 3+3 = 2 coins. Always verify that the greedy choice property holds before using a greedy approach.

mixed.question: Explain the binary search on answer technique. When should you use it?
mixed.hint: Think about searching the solution space, not the input.
mixed.answer: Binary search on the answer works when: (1) the answer lies in a known range [lo, hi], (2) you can write a function canDo(mid) that checks if a given answer is feasible, and (3) the feasibility function is monotonic (if mid works, then mid+1 also works, or vice versa). You binary search this range, checking feasibility at each midpoint. Classic examples: minimum maximum pages allocation, minimum days to make m bouquets, Koko eating bananas, and splitting array to minimize the largest sum.
mixed.explanation: The key insight is that you are not binary searching the input array -- you are binary searching the possible answer values. The feasibility check is usually a greedy simulation. Total complexity: O(n * log(answer_range)).

mixed.question:
What is the output?
int n = 8;  // 1000
cout << (n & (n - 1));
mixed.hint: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit.
mixed.explanation: 8 = 1000, 7 = 0111. 1000 & 0111 = 0. Since the result is 0, 8 is a power of 2.

mixed.hint: Two pointers: find a pair summing to 14.
Maximum of each sliding window of size 3.

mixed.question:
What is the output?
int a = 10, b = 7;
cout << (a & b) << " " << (a | b) << " " << (a ^ b);

mcqs.question: What does ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false) do?

options.B: Disables synchronization between C and C++ I/O streams for faster I/O

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. It disables the synchronization between cin/cout and scanf/printf, significantly speeding up C++ I/O. Essential for competitive programming.

mcqs.question: What is the result of n & (n - 1) when n is a power of 2?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. A power of 2 has exactly one set bit. n-1 flips that bit and sets all lower bits. AND produces 0. Example: 8 (1000) & 7 (0111) = 0.

mcqs.question: What does XOR of a number with itself give?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. a ^ a = 0 for any value a. This property is used to find the unique element in arrays where all other elements appear twice.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the two pointers technique on a sorted array of size n?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Two pointers scan the array from both ends, with each pointer moving at most n times. Total operations: O(n).

mcqs.question: What does a sliding window of fixed size k on an array of size n reduce the complexity from?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without sliding window, computing a property for each window of size k takes O(k) per window = O(n*k) total. Sliding window updates incrementally: O(1) per slide = O(n) total.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of answering a range sum query using prefix sums (after precomputation)?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. After building the prefix sum array in O(n), each range sum query is O(1): sum(l, r) = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1].

mcqs.question: What does __builtin_popcount(n) return?

options.B: The number of set bits (1s) in n

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. __builtin_popcount(n) counts the number of 1-bits in the binary representation of n. It runs in O(1) as a compiler built-in.

mcqs.question: In binary search on the answer, what does the feasibility function check?

options.B: Whether a given candidate answer is achievable under the constraints
options.C: Whether two elements sum to the target

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The feasibility function (often called canDo(mid)) checks whether a given candidate answer is achievable. It typically uses a greedy simulation to verify.

mcqs.question: Which bit operation checks if the ith bit of n is set?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Right-shift n by i positions to bring bit i to position 0, then AND with 1 to extract it. Result is 0 or 1.

mcqs.question: What is the greedy strategy for the activity selection problem?

options.A: Sort by start time, pick the earliest
options.B: Sort by end time, pick the one that ends earliest and does not overlap
options.C: Sort by duration, pick the shortest activities

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Sort activities by end time. Greedily select the next activity that starts after the current one ends. This provably maximizes the number of non-overlapping activities.

mcqs.question: For an input of size n = 10^6, which time complexity will likely pass within 1 second?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. For n = 10^6: O(n log n) = ~20 million operations (passes easily). O(n^2) = 10^12 (far too slow). O(n*sqrt(n)) = ~10^9 (borderline). O(2^n) is astronomical.

mcqs.question: What is the total number of subsets of a set with n elements?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Each element is either included or excluded, giving 2 choices per element. Total subsets: 2^n. Bitmask enumeration uses masks from 0 to 2^n - 1 to generate all subsets.

mcqs.question: What does Kadane's algorithm find?

options.D: All pairs with a given sum

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Kadane's algorithm finds the maximum sum contiguous subarray in O(n). At each position, it decides whether to extend the current subarray or start a new one: currSum = max(arr[i], currSum + arr[i]).

mcqs.question: What is the overflow-safe way to compute the midpoint in binary search?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. (lo + hi) / 2 can overflow when lo + hi exceeds INT_MAX. lo + (hi - lo) / 2 avoids this by computing the difference first, which is always non-negative and within range.

mcqs.question: What is n & (-n) for a non-zero integer n?

options.B: The lowest set bit of n (as a value, not position)
options.D: The highest set bit of n

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. n & (-n) isolates the lowest set bit. For example, 12 (1100) & -12 = 4 (0100). This trick is used in Fenwick trees (Binary Indexed Trees).

mcqs.question: What does 1 << n compute?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Left-shifting 1 by n positions gives 2^n. For example, 1 << 3 = 8 = 2^3.

codingChallenges.title: Two Pointers: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array In-Place
codingChallenges.description: Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in-place and return the new length. Use two pointers: a slow pointer for the write position and a fast pointer for scanning.
codingChallenges.constraints: O(n) time, O(1) extra space. Modify the array in-place.

codingChallenges.title: Sliding Window: Count Subarrays with Sum Exactly K
codingChallenges.description: Given an array of positive integers and a target sum K, count the number of contiguous subarrays whose sum equals exactly K using prefix sums and a hash map.
codingChallenges.constraints: O(n) time complexity. Use prefix sum + hash map pattern.

codingChallenges.title: Bit Manipulation: Find Two Non-Repeating Elements
codingChallenges.description: Given an array where every element appears twice except two elements that appear once, find those two elements using XOR in O(n) time and O(1) space.
codingChallenges.constraints: O(n) time, O(1) space. Use XOR and bit manipulation only.

codingChallenges.title: Binary Search on Answer: Koko Eating Bananas
codingChallenges.description: Koko has n piles of bananas. She can eat at most K bananas per hour from one pile. If a pile has fewer than K bananas, she finishes that pile and waits. Find the minimum K such that she finishes all bananas within H hours.
codingChallenges.constraints: Binary search on K in range [1, max(piles)]. Feasibility check: sum of ceil(pile/K) <= H.

codingChallenges.title: Kadane's Algorithm: Maximum Subarray Sum with Indices
codingChallenges.description: Find the maximum sum contiguous subarray and print the start and end indices along with the sum. Use Kadane's algorithm.

codingChallenges.title: Prefix Sum: 2D Range Sum Query
codingChallenges.description: Given a 2D matrix, precompute a 2D prefix sum so that any rectangle sum query can be answered in O(1). Compute prefix[i][j] = sum of all elements in the submatrix from (0,0) to (i-1,j-1).
codingChallenges.sampleInput: matrix = {{1,2,3},{4,5,6},{7,8,9}}, query: sum of submatrix (1,1) to (2,2)
